title: The three requirement types — and which one drives design
intro: Why the non-functional requirements — not the features — decide your architecture.

steps.title: Three kinds of requirement
steps.body:
- **Features** (functional) — *what* the system does: login, checkout, search.
- **Quality attributes** (non-functional) — *how well*: scalable, available, secure, fast.
- **Constraints** — unnegotiable limits: a regulation like GDPR, a mandated vendor.

> [!key] Features rarely drive architecture — almost any structure can be made to deliver a feature. **Quality attributes and constraints are the real architectural drivers.**

steps.title: Why features don't drive the structure
steps.body:
Systems are almost never torn down and rewritten because a *feature* is missing — features can usually be bolted onto an existing structure.

They get rewritten when they're **too slow, insecure, or can't scale** — non-functional failures that mean the architecture itself is wrong. So you design around the non-functionals.

> [!warn] 'Refactor it later' fails at system scale: infrastructure cost, months of work, rigid service contracts. The architect's highest-value work is **pre-code**.

steps.title: Make every quality attribute measurable
steps.body:
Three rules for a good quality attribute:

1. **Testable & measurable** — numbers, not adjectives. 'Fast' is meaningless; 'p99 < 200 ms' is a spec.
2. **Trade-offs** — you can't max everything; heavy encryption buys security at the cost of performance.
3. **Feasibility** — respect economics and physics (the speed of light in fibre is a hard floor).

points.label: Features
points.note: Functional — what it does. Do NOT drive architecture; most structures can deliver them.

points.label: Quality attrs
points.note: Non-functional — scalability, availability, security. DRIVE the architecture.

points.label: Constraints
points.note: Unnegotiable limits — regulation, mandated vendor. Pillars you design around.

steps.title: From requirements to APIs
steps.body:
Capture features systematically: **actors → use cases → UML sequence diagrams**.

Then the payoff: every message that **crosses the system boundary** in a sequence diagram becomes a **public API endpoint**. Requirements-gathering is how you *derive* the API contract instead of guessing it.

recap:
**Design around quality attributes and constraints — not features.**

- Separate features / quality attributes / constraints early.
- Write every quality attribute as a **measurable number** with acknowledged trade-offs.
- Run **actors → use cases → sequence diagrams**, then harvest endpoints at the system boundary.
